topic.title: Advanced color rendering
topic.p: Advanced color is a general term on Windows for any support for high dynamic range (HDR) or wide color gamut (WCG) display, allowing display of more vivid colors and higher contrast than possible on standard displays. Both HDR and WCG capable displays allow for more accurate reproduction of classic NTSC and PAL TV colors, particularly with saturated colors at the dark or light ends of the palette, and especially with the vivid colors produced by NTSC artifacting.

High dynamic range (HDR) displays can show a wider dynamic range in the displayed image, with a larger difference between the darkest and brightest colors on the screen. Most PC HDR screens are monitors, with HDR support on laptop screens being uncommon.

HDR displays are often rated by their maximum brightness, measured in nits. Low-end HDR capable displays are generally rated for 400 nits, better HDR displays at 600 nits, and the best displays at 1000 nits. There are also variations in how the high dynamic range is achieved, with better displays using edge-based lighting and the best displays using zone lighting.

While low-end 400 nit displays are generally only barely capable of HDR, the extra brightness and color gamut are still useful for emulating the look of old CRT TVs, which didn't have very good dynamic range themselves. Thus, while such displays may not be good for displaying HDR content, they can still be decent for emulation display.

A wide color gamut (WCG) capable display supports displaying colors beyond a standard display, but without high dynamic range capability. Standalone monitors supporting wide gamut but not HDR are uncommon; most displays that support only WCG but not HDR are laptop screens.

Many displays now support wider color gamut than sRGB, but not all of them support proper WCG display in Windows; some of them only support changing the color mode on the display, which essentially distorts the displayed image from the computer, which results in most programs looking oversaturated. Proper WCG displays support a 10-bit per channel (30 bits per pixel) image, which allows the computer to know the actual color space used and properly generate an image for it.

To use advanced color rendering in Altirra, the display must be enabled for advanced color in Windows. Advanced Color is supported by Windows starting with Windows 8.1, but it is not really usable until Windows 10. HDR and WCG support are both enabled in the System, Display section of Windows Settings, on a per-display basis.
HDR is enabled in a subsection under Brightness and Color, HDR. If available, a toggle will be shown to enable HDR:
Doing so will cause the screen to blank momentarily as the display mode is changed.

In HDR mode, Windows will map non-HDR content to HDR using the SDR brightness slider on the same settings page. Regular desktop program content should look roughly the same as it does with HDR disabled, with the brightness controlled by the SDR level setting. If it looks heavily distorted or really washed out, HDR is not working properly and the display colors are being distorted. This is unfortunately not uncommon. If you are using an HDMI connection for the display, try switching to a DisplayPort connection as it is sometimes more reliable.

Some displays will not support enabling HDR but will show a different mode called "HDR video streaming" as supported. This is a cheaty version of fake HDR that mainly just consists of raising the display brightness to max and can't be used here.
Wide color display is enabled under a different setting. In Windows 11, this can be enabled under Color Profile, Automatically manage color for apps:

Enabling this will cause Windows to composite the display in an HDR-like mode, allowing access to the wide color gamut of the display. This will force all non-WCG/HDR apps to render in sRGB, which may reduce their saturation compared to this setting being disabled.

Not all displays with wide color gamut support this mode. Some older professional displays, in particular, may support 30-bit color or wider color gamut modes like Adobe RGB, but not support the HDR-like signal mode needed for the Automatically manage color for apps setting.
topic.h3: Overhead of running with advanced color
topic.p: Enabling either HDR or WCG (automatically manage color for apps) causes Windows to composite the display in a more expensive mode. This increases the load on the GPU and can reduce the overall performance of the system slightly.

HDR and WCG display modes also send more data to the screen. Depending on the HDMI or DisplayPort version supported both by the screen and by the GPU, and even the cable used, the selection of display resolutions and refresh modes may be more limited when HDR or WCG is enabled.
For these reasons, it can be a good idea to only enable these settings when running a program using HDR or wide color display, and keep them disabled otherwise.

Once HDR or WCG is enabled on the display in Windows, it can then be enabled in Altirra. This is done in View, Adjust Screen Effects, Advanced Color. Direct3D 11 and hardware accelerated screen effects must also be enabled (on by default).

The Advanced Color options available will vary depending on the display capability. On a WCG-capable display, there is only an on/off toggle. When disabled, the emulator will render to plain sRGB color. When it is enabled, the emulator will render to the wide color gamut instead.

On an HDR display, options will also appear for controlling the brightness of SDR and HDR content. The main emulated computer display is controlled by the HDR slider and all of the ancillary graphics like status overlays and messages are controlled by the SDR slider. On low-end HDR displays, it's common that the HDR brightness can't be raised above about 250 nits before colors start to clamp out and distort at the high end, even though the display may support 400 nits max; this is due to color saturation. This is OK, since HDR is more of a benefit here for the wider color gamut support than the dynamic range.
